text:
To find the combined weight of the two pennies, we start by determining the weight of the 2010 penny. According to the problem, the 2010 penny is three-quarters of the weight of the 1959 penny.

The weight of the 1959 penny is given as 48 grains. We calculate the weight of the 2010 penny as follows:

\[
\text{Weight of the 2010 penny} = \frac{3}{4} \times \text{Weight of the 1959 penny} = \frac{3}{4} \times 48 \text{ grains}
\]

Next, we perform the multiplication:

\[
\frac{3}{4} \times 48 = 3 \times 12 = 36 \text{ grains}
\]

So, the 2010 penny weighs 36 grains.

Now, we find the combined weight of the two pennies by adding the weight of the 2010 penny to the weight of the 1959 penny:

\[
\text{Combined weight} = \text{Weight of the 2010 penny} + \text{Weight of the 1959 penny} = 36 \text{ grains} + 48 \text{ grains}
\]

\[
\text{Combined weight} = 84 \text{ grains}
\]

Thus, the combined weight of the two pennies is \(\boxed{84}\) grains.
